claims.claim_text: The section title presents a specific uncited outage costing $180,000.
claims.span_hint: section: The BGP Misconfiguration That Cost $180,000
claims.anchor_text: ## The BGP Misconfiguration That Cost $180,000
claims.suggested_rewrite: ## The BGP Misconfiguration That Caused a Major Outage
claims.rationale: This is a specific financial incident claim with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: A single wrong ASN caused $180,000 in lost revenue.
claims.anchor_text: One wrong number. Four characters. $180,000 in lost revenue and a very uncomfortable post-mortem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One wrong number in a BGP configuration can trigger a costly outage and a very uncomfortable post-mortem.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is teachable, but the precise loss figure is an unattributed war story.

claims.claim_text: After this specific incident, the team moved to BGPPeer CRDs through GitOps review.
claims.anchor_text: The lesson was not about blame. It was about automation: after this incident, the team moved to Calico's BGPPeer CRDs managed through GitOps, where every change was reviewed in a pull request before applying.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The lesson is about automation: managing BGP peer configuration through reviewed, declarative change control reduces the risk of manual mistakes.
claims.rationale: This extends the uncited anecdote with additional unsupported historical specifics.

claims.claim_text: Calico has been the production workhorse of Kubernetes networking since before Kubernetes was mainstream.
claims.anchor_text: While Cilium innovates with eBPF, Calico has been the production workhorse of Kubernetes networking since before Kubernetes was mainstream.
claims.suggested_rewrite: While Cilium emphasizes eBPF-first designs, Calico has long been a widely used Kubernetes networking option.
claims.rationale: This is a superlative historical positioning claim without a precise, authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: Calico does not use overlays by default and gives pods routable IPs without NAT, encapsulation, or tunnels.
claims.anchor_text: Calico does not use overlay networks by default. It assigns real, routable IP addresses to pods and distributes routes using the same protocol that runs the internet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In routed BGP deployments, Calico can avoid overlays by assigning pod IPs from routable pools and distributing reachability with BGP.
claims.rationale: The original wording is too absolute because Calico commonly runs with VXLAN or IP-in-IP depending on deployment.

claims.claim_text: Calico has the most comprehensive policy model in the ecosystem.
claims.anchor_text: It has the most comprehensive policy model in the ecosystem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It offers an extensive policy model with features beyond the core Kubernetes NetworkPolicy API.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited superlative rather than a verifiable product fact.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy is namespace-scoped, pod-focused, lacks explicit deny, and does not provide global, host/node-specific, or L7/TLS policy primitives in the API.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NetworkPolicy concept page directly describes the API's scope and current limitations.

claims.claim_text: Typha sits between the datastore and many Felix/confd clients, reducing watch fan-out and becoming important at 100+ node scale.
claims.rationale: The current Calico primary source is the Tigera docs site, which is off the present allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Tigera was founded in 2015 by engineers from Metaswitch Networks.
claims.anchor_text: Calico was originally developed by Tigera (founded in 2015 by engineers from Metaswitch Networks, a telecoms company). The BGP expertise came directly from building carrier-grade routing software -- the same technology that routes phone calls across continents now routes your Kubernetes pods.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Calico came out of engineers with deep networking and routing experience, which helps explain its strong BGP story.
claims.rationale: The dated founding and company-history specifics are not reliably backed here, and current vendor history pages describe the timeline differently.

claims.claim_text: Over 8 million nodes run Calico worldwide; it powers major banks, telecoms, government agencies, and 3 of the top 5 cloud providers; EKS launched with Calico as an early supported option.
claims.anchor_text: Over 8 million nodes run Calico worldwide, making it the most widely deployed Kubernetes networking solution. It powers clusters at major banks, telecoms, government agencies, and 3 of the top 5 cloud providers. When AWS launched EKS, Calico was one of the first supported CNI options alongside their own VPC CNI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Calico is widely deployed and is used across cloud, enterprise, and on-premises Kubernetes environments.
claims.rationale: This mixes marketing scale numbers, unnamed customer segments, and historical launch claims that are not all directly supportable from one authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: Calico's eBPF dataplane can replace kube-proxy and outperforms iptables by 30-40% in benchmarks.
claims.anchor_text: Calico's eBPF dataplane can replace kube-proxy entirely -- just like Cilium -- eliminating iptables overhead for Service routing. In benchmarks, Calico's eBPF mode matches or exceeds traditional iptables performance by 30-40%, with consistent latency regardless of the number of Services in the cluster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Calico's eBPF dataplane can replace kube-proxy and is designed to avoid the service-path scaling behavior associated with large iptables rule sets.
claims.rationale: The specific benchmark delta is not anchored to a stable primary benchmark page here.

claims.claim_text: Project Calico is named after a calico cat and the name was chosen as a networking metaphor.
claims.anchor_text: Project Calico is named after a calico cat. The project's creators wanted a name that was friendly and memorable. The calico cat's distinctive patches of different colors inspired the metaphor of networking patches -- connecting different network segments into one cohesive fabric.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Project Calico uses cat-themed branding and a memorable name.
claims.rationale: The naming-story details are uncited project lore rather than a sourced technical fact.

claims.claim_text: The calico/node container runs Felix, BIRD, and confd.
claims.span_hint: architecture diagram and section: Component Deep Dive
claims.rationale: Calico's primary documentation directly states this, but it is on an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: The operator deploys Typha when needed, and Typha is required above 200 nodes.
claims.anchor_text: The Tigera operator automatically deploys Typha when needed. For clusters over 200 nodes, Typha is not optional -- it is required for stability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Operator-managed installations deploy Typha, and large clusters should use it to reduce API-server fan-out.
claims.rationale: The exact 200-node cutoff is not directly backed here, and current docs frame Typha guidance differently.
RFC 6996 is the authoritative source for these private-use ASN ranges.

claims.claim_text: Calico IPAM uses /26 blocks by default for IPv4, groups routes by block, and borrowing an IP creates a more specific /32 route.
claims.span_hint: section: IP Pools; section: IP Borrowing
claims.rationale: The IPPool resource docs directly back the block-size and borrowed-address routing behavior, but they are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Namespace-level cni.projectcalico.org/ipv4pools annotations can steer pod IP assignment to a specific pool.
claims.rationale: The CNI plugin reference documents namespace and pod annotations for pool selection, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: HostEndpoint resources let Calico apply policy to host interfaces.
claims.rationale: The HostEndpoint resource docs directly support this capability, but they are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Calico tiers provide ordered policy evaluation, and Pass moves evaluation to the next tier.
claims.rationale: The Tier resource docs explicitly describe ordering and Pass semantics, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Calico application-layer policy is optional, HTTP-based, and restricted to ingress allow rules.
claims.rationale: The NetworkPolicy reference documents current L7 restrictions, but the primary doc host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module presents DNS-based policy as a standard Calico capability.
claims.anchor_text: ### DNS-Based Policies
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### DNS-Based Policies (Commercial Editions)
claims.rationale: Current product docs place DNS/FQDN-based policy in Calico's commercial feature set rather than general OSS capability.

claims.claim_text: Calico eBPF replaces kube-proxy and preserves external source IP without externalTrafficPolicy: Local.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Use eBPF vs iptables
claims.rationale: Current Calico eBPF docs directly describe kube-proxy replacement and external source IP behavior, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: eBPF should be used when running Linux kernel 5.3+ and ideally 5.8+.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Use eBPF vs iptables
claims.anchor_text: ✓ Running Linux kernel 5.3+ (ideally 5.8+)
claims.suggested_rewrite: ✓ Running a kernel and distribution supported by your current Calico release
claims.rationale: Current enablement docs describe support in terms of specific supported kernels and distributions, not this older blanket threshold.

claims.claim_text: HostEndpoint policies are limited in eBPF mode.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Use eBPF vs iptables
claims.anchor_text: ✓ Need HostEndpoint policies (limited in eBPF mode)
claims.suggested_rewrite: ✓ Need to verify current HostEndpoint support and limitations for your Calico release before choosing eBPF
claims.rationale: Current docs no longer support this broad limitation statement as written.

claims.claim_text: eBPF benchmark row: 18,500 vs 26,200 connections/sec at 1000 Services.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Numbers table row Connections/sec
claims.anchor_text: | **Connections/sec** (1000 Services) | 18,500 | 26,200 | +42% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Connections/sec** (high service count) | workload-dependent | often higher with eBPF | varies |
claims.rationale: These exact benchmark values need a directly matching benchmark source, which is not established here.

claims.claim_text: eBPF benchmark row: P99 latency drops from 4.2ms to 1.8ms at 1000 Services.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Numbers table row P99 latency
claims.anchor_text: | **P99 latency** (1000 Services) | 4.2ms | 1.8ms | -57% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **P99 latency** (high service count) | higher | often lower with eBPF | varies |
claims.rationale: The exact latency values and percentage need a stable benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: eBPF benchmark row: idle CPU per node drops from 2.1% to 1.4%.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Numbers table row CPU per node
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **CPU per node** (idle) | environment-dependent | environment-dependent | varies |
claims.rationale: These percentage figures are benchmark-specific and uncited.

claims.claim_text: eBPF benchmark row: service rule update time drops from 120ms to 8ms.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Numbers table row Rule update time
claims.anchor_text: | **Rule update time** (adding a Service) | 120ms | 8ms | -93% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Rule update time** (adding a Service) | rule-count dependent | typically faster | varies |
claims.rationale: The exact timing claim is not tied to a citable benchmark page here.

claims.claim_text: eBPF benchmark row: latency scaling is O(1) vs O(n) from 10 to 10,000 Services.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Numbers table row Latency scaling
claims.anchor_text: | **Latency scaling** (10→10,000 Services) | Linear increase | Constant | O(1) vs O(n) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Latency scaling** (service growth) | rule-count dependent | flatter lookup costs | architecture dependent |
claims.rationale: The exact table claim blends theory and benchmark output without a directly cited benchmark artifact.

claims.claim_text: Service mesh mTLS adds 50-100MB memory overhead per pod.
claims.anchor_text: **Service mesh mTLS**: Adds a sidecar to every pod, 50-100MB memory overhead per pod, complex certificate management
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Service mesh mTLS**: Adds a sidecar to every pod and introduces extra memory and certificate-management overhead
claims.rationale: The memory range is an uncited benchmark-style claim that varies significantly by mesh and configuration.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard has ~3-5% CPU overhead and zero configuration per pod.
claims.anchor_text: **WireGuard**: Kernel-level, ~3-5% CPU overhead, zero configuration per pod
claims.suggested_rewrite: **WireGuard**: Kernel-level encryption with no per-pod sidecars and comparatively simple operational setup
claims.rationale: The exact CPU-overhead figure is a benchmark claim without a directly cited source.

claims.claim_text: Calico automatically creates and manages WireGuard tunnels between nodes and exposes wireguardEnabled / wireguardEnabledV6 controls.
claims.rationale: The WireGuard feature docs directly back this claim, but the primary doc host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard benchmark row: throughput drops from 9.4 Gbps to 8.9 Gbps.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Overhead table row Throughput
claims.anchor_text: | **Throughput** (TCP) | 9.4 Gbps | 8.9 Gbps | ~5% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Throughput** (TCP) | workload-dependent | usually somewhat lower | varies |
claims.rationale: These exact throughput numbers require a matching benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard benchmark row: latency rises from 0.12ms to 0.14ms.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Overhead table row Latency
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Latency** (P50) | baseline dependent | usually slightly higher | varies |
claims.rationale: The exact latency deltas are uncited benchmark specifics.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard benchmark row: CPU per node rises from 1.4% to 2.1%.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Overhead table row CPU per node
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **CPU per node** | environment-dependent | usually somewhat higher | varies |
claims.rationale: The exact CPU values are benchmark-specific and unsupported here.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard adds 60 bytes of MTU overhead.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Overhead table row MTU overhead
claims.rationale: The Calico MTU guide directly lists WireGuard header overhead, but it is hosted off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says Cilium BGP support is limited via MetalLB.
claims.span_hint: section: Comparison: Calico vs Cilium vs Flannel, row BGP support
claims.anchor_text: | **BGP support** | Native, first-class | Limited (via MetalLB) | None |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **BGP support** | Native, first-class | Supported, but with a different architecture | None |
claims.rationale: Current Cilium documentation includes a BGP Control Plane, so this row is outdated as written.

claims.claim_text: The closing quote says BGP has routed the Internet since 1994 and that there is no more proven foundation for production networking.
claims.anchor_text: "BGP has been routing the internet since 1994. Calico brings that same battle-tested protocol to every pod in your cluster. There is no more proven foundation for production networking."
claims.suggested_rewrite: "BGP has been used in Internet routing for decades, and Calico applies that routing model to Kubernetes networking."
claims.rationale: The 1994 date is citable, but the uncited superlative about 'no more proven foundation' is not.

further_reading.title: Project Calico GitHub Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream project entry point for releases, code, and high-level feature references.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Network Policies
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative baseline for what the core Kubernetes NetworkPolicy API does and does not provide.

further_reading.title: RFC 4456: BGP Route Reflection
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical reference for the route-reflector model used to avoid full-mesh BGP scaling problems.

notes: As of 2026-04-20, several comparison and eBPF guidance lines are outdated relative to current Calico/Cilium docs. Many Calico feature claims are otherwise supportable from docs.tigera.io, but that host is off the current allowlist, so they are marked needs_allowlist_expansion.
